name: Chalchiuhtlicue
description: Chalchiuhtlicue, meaning "She of the Jade Skirt," is a prominent Aztec goddess associated with water, rivers, seas, streams, storms, and baptism. She is considered a vital life-giving force, responsible for the fertility of the earth and the sustenance of humankind. Her association with water extends beyond its life-giving properties; she also embodies the destructive potential of floods and storms. Chalchiuhtlicue is often depicted wearing a jade skirt, symbolizing preciousness and purity, and is sometimes shown with a flowing stream of water emanating from her skirt, signifying her role as a provider of sustenance. She is also associated with childbirth and is invoked for the safe delivery of children. As a powerful and complex deity, Chalchiuhtlicue represents the duality of water, both its life-sustaining and life-threatening aspects, reflecting the Aztec understanding of the natural world.
subtitle: Goddess of Water and Fertility
titles: Lady of the Precious Green Liquid

keyMyths.title: The Great Flood
keyMyths.description: Chalchiuhtlicue presided over the Fourth Sun, Nahui-Atl (Four Water). This era ended with a devastating flood, caused either by Chalchiuhtlicue herself or by Tlaloc, the rain god, under her command. The flood transformed humans into fish, signifying the destructive power inherent in water and the cyclical nature of Aztec cosmology. This myth underscores the Aztec understanding of the world as subject to periodic destruction and renewal.
keyMyths.source: Historia de los Mexicanos por sus pinturas

keyMyths.title: Sacrifices to Chalchiuhtlicue
keyMyths.description: In order to appease Chalchiuhtlicue and ensure continued access to fresh water, the Aztecs performed human sacrifices, often drowning victims in bodies of water sacred to the goddess. These sacrifices were intended to maintain the balance between humans and the natural world and to ensure the continued fertility of the land. The victims were often chosen for their beauty and purity, reflecting the preciousness associated with water.

keyMyths.title: Chalchiuhtlicue and Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Pregnant women often invoked Chalchiuhtlicue to ensure a safe and successful childbirth. Her association with water and its life-giving properties made her a natural patron of expectant mothers. Prayers and offerings were made to the goddess, seeking her protection and guidance during the vulnerable period of pregnancy and delivery. This reflects the crucial role of women in Aztec society and the importance of ensuring the continuation of the lineage.
